sections.title: Pictures of the Sky
sections.content_summary: The chapter opens by inviting students to observe how the sky looks different at various times of day — its colour changes from morning to afternoon to night, the Sun's apparent colour shifts, and the Moon's shape seems to change over time. Students observe that the Sun is the brightest object in the sky, so bright that other stars are invisible when it is present, and it provides light and heat. The Sun appears to rise in the morning, move overhead at noon, and descend on the opposite side in the evening. Students are asked to record their sky observations in a table and note changes in animal and plant behaviour across the day.

tasks.task_text: Draw a picture of the sky as it appears in the morning, afternoon and at night, in the spaces given below.
Write down what you observe in the sky during the morning, afternoon and at night, in the table given below. A few boxes are filled for you. You may expand your table by adding new points.
Share your observations with your classmates and compare them.
Have you observed any change in the activities, and the behaviour of animals and plants at different times of a day? Discuss these changes with your friends and make a list.

Have you observed the changes during sunrise and sunset? When the Sun rises: 1. How does the sky look? 2. What do the birds and animals do? 3. What changes do we find at home? When the Sun sets: 1. How does the sky look? 2. What do the birds and animals do? 3. What changes do we find at home?

sections.title: The Shadows
sections.content_summary: This section explores how shadows form and change throughout the day due to the Sun's apparent movement from East to West. Morning and evening shadows are long and fall in opposite directions, while noon shadows are shortest because the Sun is overhead. Students learn that the closer an object is to the light source, the larger the shadow, and that the direction of the shadow follows the direction of the light source. The section connects shadow behaviour to the Sun's apparent east-to-west arc across the sky. An IKS connection is provided through the sundial: ancient people used shadows to tell time, and Raja Jai Singh of Jaipur built the Jantar Mantar observatories across five Indian cities, featuring the Samrat Yantra sundial that accurately tells time from shadows. Shadow puppetry is noted as a long-practised Indian art form.

tasks.task_text: Take a torch and a stick. Close the doors and windows to make the room dark. Hold the stick in one place and turn on the torch. Shine the torchlight on the stick. Observe the changes in the shadow of the stick, in the following situations: (a) Move the stick back and forth. (b) Change the direction of the torch.
Draw the position of the Sun and corresponding shadows in the images given below.

sections.title: Night Sky
sections.content_summary: After sunset the sky fills with thousands of twinkling stars that form various patterns; students are encouraged to observe and draw patterns they see, including traditional Indian patterns such as Saptarshi (Seven Sages). The Moon is the biggest apparent object in the night sky and its shape changes across nights: a full circle called Poornima, a crescent like the letter 'C', a half-moon, or it may be invisible altogether on Amavasya (new Moon). Ancient peoples used the Moon's changing shape to count days, giving rise to the earliest calendars. Many Indian and other festivals are timed by the Moon — Diwali on Amavasya, Rakshabandhan/Guru Poornima/Buddha Poornima on Poornima, and Eid on the first crescent after a new Moon.

tasks.task_text: Observe the Moon in the sky and draw the shapes of the Moon you have observed on different nights.
Is there a pattern to the way the shape of the Moon changes?
Are there days when the Moon is not visible in the sky?
Do you celebrate any festivals in your family or community related to the Sun or the Moon? What do you call them in your language?

sections.title: India's Chandrayaan Mission
sections.content_summary: This section briefly connects to prior learning (Grade 3 English and Hindi textbooks) about India's Chandrayaan mission, noting that on 23 August 2023 the Vikram Lander successfully landed on the Moon and collected data about its surface. A Let us reflect exercise then consolidates the chapter's learning through four tasks requiring students to compare day/night, explain shadow behaviour, enquire scientifically about the Sun's motion, and express sky observations creatively through poetry and riddles.

tasks.task_text: Give two differences between: (a) Day and night (b) Sky in the morning and in the evening (c) Our activities during the day and at night (d) Activities of animals during the day and at night.

A teacher said that the Sun appears to move from East to West during the day. Meera is curious about this statement and has some questions for her teacher. List at least two questions that she can ask.

Suppose you stand at the same spot during the morning and the afternoon. In the morning, your shadow points in one direction, while in the afternoon it points in another. Why? Would the length of your shadow be the same at both times?
Write your own poem inspired by the beauty of the sky.
Complete the riddle below and answer it— Only one colour, but not one size / Appears with light, looks very nice

dual_strand.natural: Sun's apparent daily motion from East to West and its effects on shadow length and direction; Moon's changing phases across the lunar cycle
dual_strand.human_cultural: Cultural timekeeping and festival calendars tied to Moon phases (Diwali, Eid, Rakshabandhan, Pongal, Chhath Puja, Karva Chauth); Indian astronomical heritage — Jantar Mantar observatories and the Samrat Yantra sundial built by Raja Jai Singh; shadow puppetry as a traditional Indian art form
